subject: Governance
trap_factor: Eusebio is associated with football, not basketball, and Wally Gout is a cricketer not a tennis player — options mix these to create confusion. Mark Spitz being a swimmer is well-known but Laver's sport can be confused.
elimination_hint: Mark Spitz=Swimming is a strong anchor; Rod Laver=Lawn Tennis is also well-known. Eliminate options where these are wrong.
factual_hooks: Wally Gout: Australian cricketer
Eusebio: Portuguese football legend
Mark Spitz: 7-gold Olympic swimmer 1972
contrast_with: Eusebio (football) vs Laver (tennis)

subject: Polity
trap_factor: The 98th Amendment is obscure; candidates may confuse it with the 99th (NJAC) or 97th amendment. Option B (National Judicial Commission) relates to a later amendment.
elimination_hint: The 99th Amendment (2014) established NJAC; 98th (2012) related to Karnataka/Hyderabad-Karnataka region — narrow down by recalling amendment sequences.
factual_hooks: 98th Amendment Act 2012: National Judicial Commission/Hyderabad-Karnataka
99th Amendment: NJAC 2014
contrast_with: 98th vs 99th Amendment

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Candidates may confuse area rankings if they assume West Bengal is larger due to historical significance or higher population density.
elimination_hint: Chhattisgarh formed in 2000 from a large portion of MP and is clearly larger in area (~135,000 sq km) vs West Bengal (~88,000 sq km). Both statements are correct.
factual_hooks: Chhattisgarh area ~135,192 sq km; West Bengal ~88,752 sq km
West Bengal population far exceeds Chhattisgarh per 2001 Census
contrast_with: Area vs population ranking of states

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Statement 1 says Silent Valley is in Nallamalai range — it is actually in the Nilgiri Hills of Kerala, not the Nallamalai range (which is in Andhra Pradesh), making it incorrect.
elimination_hint: Statement 1 is clearly wrong — Silent Valley is in Kerala's Nilgiri Hills, not Nallamalai. Eliminate options containing Statement 1; the correct answer includes only 2 and 3.
factual_hooks: Silent Valley NP: Palakkad, Kerala — Nilgiri hills, not Nallamalai
Kunthi river originates in Silent Valley rainforests
contrast_with: Nallamalai Hills (AP) vs Nilgiri Hills (Kerala)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Candidates may doubt whether Cowpox is infectious in milching animals or only relevant to humans historically; all four listed diseases are infectious/contagious.
elimination_hint: All four — Foot and Mouth, Anthrax, Black Quarter, Cowpox — are well-known infectious diseases of cattle. Select 'All of the above' (D).
factual_hooks: FMD, Anthrax, Black Quarter, Cowpox: all infectious cattle diseases
Anthrax is zoonotic — transmissible to humans
contrast_with: Infectious vs nutritional/metabolic diseases of cattle

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Kothagudem (thermal) is in Telangana/AP, Raichur is in Karnataka, Mettur is in Tamil Nadu, Wanakbori is in Gujarat — candidates confuse Raichur with AP and Kothagudem with Karnataka.
elimination_hint: Anchor on Mettur=Tamil Nadu (well-known dam) and Wanakbori=Gujarat; then Kothagudem=AP and Raichur=Karnataka follows naturally.
factual_hooks: Kothagudem thermal plant: Andhra Pradesh/Telangana
Mettur dam and power station: Tamil Nadu
Wanakbori thermal: Gujarat
contrast_with: Thermal vs hydro power stations

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: The dog covers multiple back-and-forth trips; candidates mistakenly try to calculate each trip separately instead of using total time × dog speed.
elimination_hint: Find time for Rahul to catch Aryan: gap=200m at closing speed 10m/min = 20 min. Dog speed × time = 60 × 20 = 1200 m.
factual_hooks: Dog problem shortcut: total time × dog speed
Rahul catches Aryan in 20 minutes
contrast_with: Series summation approach vs time-speed shortcut

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Thai (Thal) and Manuguru heavy water plants are often confused with atomic power plants; their state locations are frequently swapped in options.
elimination_hint: Kaiga=Karnataka is well-known; Kakrapar=Gujarat is a strong anchor. Thal (Thai)=Maharashtra, Manuguru heavy water=AP confirms option D.
factual_hooks: Kaiga Nuclear Power Plant: Karnataka
Kakrapar Atomic Power Station: Gujarat
Thal (Thai) Heavy Water Plant: Maharashtra
prerequisite_concepts: Heavy water plants vs power plants
contrast_with: Heavy water plants vs nuclear power stations

subject: Internal Security
trap_factor: Armoured Corps Centre and School is in Ahmednagar (Maharashtra), not Jabalpur — Jabalpur is associated with Infantry School, causing confusion.
elimination_hint: The incorrect pair is Armoured Corps Centre=Jabalpur; it is actually in Ahmednagar. Army Medical Corps=Lucknow is correct.
factual_hooks: Armoured Corps Centre and School: Ahmednagar, not Jabalpur
Infantry School: Mhow (Madhya Pradesh)
contrast_with: Armoured Corps (Ahmednagar) vs Infantry School (Mhow)

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: Some candidates may confuse Bandung with the location of other Cold War conferences in Egypt or Thailand; NAM's founding conference is a recurring UPSC topic.
elimination_hint: Bandung Conference (1955) was held in Bandung, Indonesia — a standard fact. Eliminate Thailand, Egypt, Philippines directly.
factual_hooks: Bandung Conference 1955: Bandung, Indonesia
Led to establishment of Non-Aligned Movement
contrast_with: Bandung (1955) vs Belgrade (1961, first NAM Summit)
contemporary_relevance: NAM's relevance debated amid multipolarity and India's strategic autonomy policy.

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Candidates confuse placing 6 balls in 9 squares (with at least one per row) with simpler distribution problems; overcounting or undercounting rows is common.
elimination_hint: Each row has 3 squares; choose one square per row for guaranteed coverage (3×3×3=27 ways), then distribute remaining 3 balls freely among all 9 squares giving 9³=729... approach carefully using inclusion-exclusion or structured logic giving 81.
factual_hooks: Answer: 81 (3^4 approach)
Each row must have at least one of 6 balls in 9 squares
contrast_with: With vs without restriction distribution

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Tirupur is sometimes confused with Coimbatore (gems/textiles hub) or Surat (gems); candidates may pick gems and jewellery.
elimination_hint: Tirupur = 'Knitwear Capital of India' — this is a well-known industrial geography fact. Option C (Knitted garments) is unambiguous.
factual_hooks: Tirupur: Knitwear/Knitted garment export hub of India in Tamil Nadu
One of India's largest textile export centres
contrast_with: Tirupur (knitwear) vs Surat (gems) vs Agra (leather)

subject: Governance
trap_factor: 2004 Davis Cup Final involved Spain vs USA — a dated sports fact that may be confused with other years' finals involving Switzerland or Australia.
elimination_hint: Recall that 2004 Davis Cup Final was Spain vs USA (Spain won); Roger Federer (Switzerland) dominated individual tennis but Davis Cup is a different competition.
factual_hooks: 2004 Davis Cup Final: Spain defeated USA
Spain has been a consistent Davis Cup powerhouse
contrast_with: Individual Grand Slams vs Davis Cup team event

subject: History
trap_factor: Statement 1 (Warren Hastings establishing police force) is incorrect — it was Cornwallis who reorganized the police system; candidates may accept it as true.
elimination_hint: Statement 1 is wrong (Cornwallis, not Hastings, reformed police). Statement 2 (Supreme Court at Calcutta via Regulating Act 1773) and Statement 3 (IPC 1860) are both correct. Answer is B.
factual_hooks: Regulating Act 1773: established Supreme Court at Calcutta
IPC enacted 1860 under Lord Canning
Cornwallis reformed police, not Warren Hastings
contrast_with: Warren Hastings reforms vs Cornwallis reforms

subject: History
trap_factor: Milinda (Menander) was a Greco-Bactrian king of the 2nd century BCE, not a contemporary of Buddha (6th-5th century BCE) — candidates may confuse Buddhist-era kings.
elimination_hint: Bimbisara, Gautama Buddha, and Prasenjit were all contemporaries in the 6th-5th century BCE. Milinda/Menander belongs to the 2nd century BCE — over 3 centuries later.
factual_hooks: Milinda = Menander, Greco-Bactrian king, 2nd century BCE
Bimbisara, Buddha, Prasenjit: 6th–5th century BCE contemporaries
contrast_with: Mauryan era vs pre-Mauryan era personalities

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Pneumoconiosis literally means 'dusty lung' — candidates may associate it with glass industry (silicosis) or tanneries, but coal mining is the classic cause.
elimination_hint: Pneumoconiosis in coal miners = 'Black Lung disease' (coal dust). Silicosis affects glass/stone workers but the generic term pneumoconiosis is most strongly associated with coal mining.
factual_hooks: Pneumoconiosis: occupational lung disease from dust inhalation
Coal miners' pneumoconiosis = Black Lung disease
contrast_with: Pneumoconiosis (coal dust) vs Silicosis (silica dust) vs Asbestosis (asbestos)

subject: History
factual_hooks: India's early consolidation: Sardar Patel's integration of 562 princely states
First Five Year Plan (1951) set economic foundation
Non-Aligned Movement (1961) shaped foreign policy
contrast_with: British colonial economy vs planned post-independence economy
mains_intro: India's post-independence consolidation across polity, economy, education and international relations reflects a deliberate nation-building project under visionary leadership.
mains_body_points: Polity: Integration of 562 princely states under Sardar Patel; adoption of Constitution 1950; establishment of democratic institutions
Economy: Land reforms, abolition of zamindari; First Five Year Plan (1951); establishment of PSUs and mixed economy framework
Education: University Education Commission (1948-49); IITs established; emphasis on scientific temper and technical education
International Relations: NAM, Panchsheel (1954), Bandung Conference (1955); India as a voice of newly decolonised nations
mains_conclusion: India's early post-independence consolidation laid the institutional, economic and diplomatic foundations that continue to shape the nation's trajectory.
contemporary_relevance: Lessons from India's early consolidation inform current debates on federalism, economic planning, and strategic autonomy.

subject: Ethics
factual_hooks: Empathy: understanding others' perspectives and feelings
Compassion: empathy + motivation to help
contrast_with: Intellectual competency vs emotional competency in civil services
mains_themes: Empathy and compassion as civil service virtues
mains_intro: Beyond intellectual and moral qualities, empathy and compassion enable civil servants to make humane, contextually informed decisions that serve diverse citizen needs.
mains_body_points: Empathy helps officials understand ground-level realities — e.g., a DM empathizing with drought-hit farmers to design targeted relief
Compassion converts empathy into action — e.g., fast-tracking disability certificates for terminally ill applicants
These qualities reduce bureaucratic rigidity and enable discretionary justice within legal frameworks
Illustrations: Collector suspending rules to admit injured child to hospital; officer expediting widow pension without undue documentation
mains_conclusion: Empathy and compassion humanise governance, enabling civil servants to bridge the gap between policy intent and citizen experience.
contemporary_relevance: Citizen-centric governance frameworks like SMART governance demand empathetic administrative culture.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Statement 1 says the Earth's magnetic axis is inclined at 23° to geographic axis — the actual inclination is about 11.5°, not 23°; 23.5° is the axial tilt from orbital plane.
elimination_hint: Statement 1 is wrong (23° is Earth's axial tilt, not magnetic axis inclination). Statement 2 and 3 are correct. Answer is B (2 and 3 only).
factual_hooks: Earth's magnetic axis inclined ~11.5° from geographic axis, not 23°
Magnetic pole in northern hemisphere: Ellesmere Island/northern Canada peninsula
prerequisite_concepts: Difference between magnetic and geographic poles
contrast_with: Geographic poles vs magnetic poles vs geomagnetic poles

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Candidates may choose 'social democracy' or 'Gandhian democracy' as partial answers; DPSP's purpose is specifically to establish both social AND economic democracy.
elimination_hint: DPSP is explicitly meant to establish 'social and economic democracy' complementing the political democracy of Fundamental Rights. Answer is D.
factual_hooks: DPSPs: establish social and economic democracy
DPSP not justiciable but fundamental to governance
contrast_with: Fundamental Rights (justiciable) vs DPSP (non-justiciable)

subject: History
trap_factor: Candidates may confuse Poona Sarvajanik Sabha with the Indian Association or Deccan Association given their similar reform-oriented nature.
elimination_hint: The Poona Sarvajanik Sabha (1870) was one of the earliest political organizations petitioning British Parliament. Its 1875 petition for direct representation is a distinguishing fact.
factual_hooks: Poona Sarvajanik Sabha (1870): petitioned House of Commons 1875 for Indian representation
Founded by Mahadev Govind Ranade
contrast_with: Poona Sarvajanik Sabha vs Indian Association vs Deccan Association

subject: International Relations
factual_hooks: South China Sea: overlapping claims by China, Vietnam, Philippines, Malaysia, Brunei, Taiwan
UNCLOS vs China's 9-dash line dispute
contrast_with: Legal (UNCLOS) vs China's historical claim approach
mains_themes: Geopolitical significance of South China Sea
mains_intro: The South China Sea, straddling critical trade routes and rich in resources, has become a flashpoint of 21st century geopolitical contestation.
mains_body_points: Strategic importance: 30% of global trade transits through it; abundant hydrocarbon reserves
Overlapping claims: China's 9-dash line vs UNCLOS-based claims of Vietnam, Philippines, Malaysia
2016 Permanent Court of Arbitration ruling against China's claims — rejected by Beijing
India's stakes: freedom of navigation, energy security, ASEAN partnerships, and countering Chinese assertiveness
US Freedom of Navigation Operations (FONOPs) add great power dimension
mains_conclusion: The South China Sea exemplifies the tension between revisionist claims and rules-based international order, with India's strategic interests demanding active diplomatic engagement.
contemporary_relevance: South China Sea tensions remain active with Chinese island-building and increased US-China rivalry post-2016.

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Candidates may choose Right to Equality (Article 14) thinking it is foundational, or Right to Freedom of Religion given Ambedkar's personal philosophy.
elimination_hint: Ambedkar called Right to Constitutional Remedies (Article 32) the 'heart and soul' — it gives effect to all other rights. This is a direct quote, unambiguous.
factual_hooks: Ambedkar: Article 32 (Right to Constitutional Remedies) = 'heart and soul of Constitution'
Article 32 allows Supreme Court to enforce Fundamental Rights
contrast_with: Article 32 (SC) vs Article 226 (HC) — both provide remedies but differ in scope

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Candidates may calculate only the floor diagonal (√(12²+9²)=15m) and forget to account for height, giving 15 as the answer instead of 17.
elimination_hint: 3D diagonal formula: √(l²+b²+h²) = √(144+81+64) = √289 = 17 m. The longest pole = space diagonal.
factual_hooks: 3D diagonal formula: √(l²+b²+h²)
Space diagonal = longest pole in a cuboid room
related_concepts: Volume and surface area of cuboid
contrast_with: Floor diagonal (2D) vs space diagonal (3D)

subject: Ethics
factual_hooks: Caste discrimination in midday meal scheme: documented real issue in Indian schools
Article 17: Abolition of untouchability
contrast_with: Legal solutions vs social persuasion approaches to caste discrimination
mains_themes: Role of Sarpanch as change agent
mains_intro: As Sarpanch, addressing caste-based food discrimination requires balancing legal authority, community sensitivity, and long-term attitudinal change.
mains_body_points: Immediate: Hold gram sabha meeting; sensitize parents about constitutional values (Article 17) and benefits of Midday Meal
Engage local school management committee, NGOs, and district administration
Involve religious and community leaders to counter regressive social norms
Responsibilities: Panchayat (enforce law), School (awareness), NGOs (social mobilization), Government (monitoring)
Long-term: Inter-caste community events; recognition of children who lead by example
mains_conclusion: Sustainable change requires both enforcement of constitutional rights and community-owned transformation of discriminatory attitudes.
contemporary_relevance: Caste discrimination in schools remains a live issue; NHRC and NCPCR have documented such violations.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Statement 1 (longer wavelength scattered more) is wrong — it's shorter wavelengths that scatter more (Rayleigh scattering); Statement 2 about light speed in water is incorrect (it's ~0.75c not 0.95c).
elimination_hint: Statements 3 (radio waves from oscillating currents) and 4 (Doppler effect for speed detection) are both correct. Statements 1 and 2 are wrong — eliminate all options not including 3 and 4 only.
factual_hooks: Shorter wavelengths scatter more (Rayleigh scattering) — sky is blue
Doppler effect used in speed guns by police
Radio waves produced by oscillating electric currents
contrast_with: Scattering (wavelength dependent) vs reflection (angle dependent)

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Candidates may guess Ministry of Railways since railway zones are involved, or President since Parliament committees are often associated with President's approval.
elimination_hint: Parliamentary Consultative Committees are constituted by Ministry of Parliamentary Affairs — a specific institutional fact about Parliament-executive interface.
factual_hooks: Consultative Committees of Parliament: constituted by Ministry of Parliamentary Affairs
These are informal advisory bodies, not standing committees
contrast_with: Consultative Committees vs Standing Committees vs Select Committees

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Candidates may confuse monogamy with territorial behavior; deer and seals appear in mating-related questions but are polygamous.
elimination_hint: Wolves are known for lifelong pair bonding (monogamy) in packs. Walrus, Seal, Deer are polygamous. Select Wolf (A).
factual_hooks: Wolves: monogamous, mate for life within pack structure
Walrus and Deer: polygamous
contrast_with: Monogamous (wolf, swan) vs polygamous (deer, seal) animals

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Candidates may confuse total distance from start vs gap between them at start, leading to calculating 7.5 km instead of 5 km.
elimination_hint: Relative speed of car over bus = 50-30 = 20 km/hr. Time to catch = 15 min = 0.25 hr. Initial gap = 20 × 0.25 = 5 km.
factual_hooks: Relative speed = speed difference for same-direction objects
Gap = relative speed × time
contrast_with: Relative speed (same direction) vs closing speed (opposite direction)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Statement 1 says ocean currents are 'slow-surface movement' — deep ocean currents (thermohaline) are also major currents, so this partial definition might seem wrong but in classical context refers to surface currents.
elimination_hint: All four statements are broadly correct in standard textbook context: currents maintain heat balance, driven by winds, affected by ocean configuration. Answer is D (all four).
factual_hooks: Ocean currents driven by: winds, Earth's rotation (Coriolis), temperature, salinity
Ocean currents maintain Earth's heat balance — warm currents carry heat poleward
contrast_with: Surface currents (wind-driven) vs deep currents (thermohaline)

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Options A (Planning Commission accountable to Parliament — incorrect, it was advisory) and D (NDC composition — Finance Minister not typically the only minister) are plausible distractors.
elimination_hint: Option B is correct: President can promulgate ordinance only when at least one House of Parliament is not in session — not necessarily both. This is a precise constitutional fact (Article 123).
factual_hooks: Article 123: Ordinance when at least one House not in session
No minimum age for SC judge — Article 124 only specifies qualifications
contrast_with: Ordinance (when Parliament not in session) vs Delegated legislation (during session)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: The sequence of rivers from north to south is frequently confused; Pennar flows south of Krishna but north of Cauvery, and Subarnarekha is northernmost.
elimination_hint: Correct sequence N to S: Subarnarekha → Mahanadi → Godavari → Krishna → Pennar → Cauvery → Vaigai. Match with option A.
factual_hooks: East-flowing peninsular rivers N→S: Subarnarekha, Mahanadi, Godavari, Krishna, Pennar, Cauvery, Vaigai
All drain into Bay of Bengal
contrast_with: East-flowing (Bay of Bengal) vs west-flowing rivers (Arabian Sea)

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Parimarjan Negi is associated with Chess, not Billiards (Pankaj Advani) or Swimming — candidates confuse Indian sports prodigies.
elimination_hint: Parimarjan Negi became India's youngest International Master in Chess at age 13. This is a direct factual recall.
factual_hooks: Parimarjan Negi: Indian Chess Grandmaster and International Master
Became youngest IM at 13 years old
contrast_with: Chess (Negi) vs Billiards (Pankaj Advani) vs Swimming (Virdhawal Khade)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Candidates may confuse FMD causation — some assume it is bacterial since symptoms resemble bacterial infections; it is caused by a virus (Aphthovirus).
elimination_hint: Foot-and-Mouth Disease is caused by a virus (Aphthovirus, family Picornaviridae). This is a standard biology/veterinary fact.
factual_hooks: FMD: caused by Aphthovirus (RNA virus), family Picornaviridae
Highly contagious disease of cloven-hoofed animals
contrast_with: Viral (FMD) vs Bacterial (Anthrax) vs Protozoal (Surra) cattle diseases

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: This is a highly specific current affairs fact from 2001 about India-Tajikistan relations; candidates may confuse the purpose with defence or technology cooperation.
elimination_hint: In 2001, Tajikistan was experiencing severe drought; India's $5 million grant for drought relief aligns with this humanitarian context.
factual_hooks: India-Tajikistan 2001: $5 million drought relief grant
Tajikistan suffered severe droughts in early 2000s
contrast_with: Humanitarian aid vs strategic investment in neighbourhood

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Macedonia is landlocked and does not touch the Adriatic; candidates may include it thinking the Balkans region broadly borders the sea. Bosnia Herzegovina has only a tiny coastline (Neum).
elimination_hint: Albania and Croatia clearly border the Adriatic; Bosnia has a tiny Adriatic coast (Neum). Macedonia is landlocked. Answer is B (1, 2, and 3).
factual_hooks: Adriatic Sea bordering countries: Italy, Slovenia, Croatia, Bosnia-Herz, Montenegro, Albania
Macedonia (North Macedonia) is landlocked
contrast_with: Adriatic Sea access (Albania, Croatia) vs landlocked Balkans (Macedonia, Serbia)

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Candidates may confuse the order; ICICI is often thought to have been established after IDBI due to its commercial bank prominence, but IFCI was first (1948).
elimination_hint: IFCI (1948) → ICICI (1955) → IDBI (1964) → NABARD (1982): chronological sequence is A (1,2,3,4).
factual_hooks: IFCI: 1948 (oldest DFI)
ICICI: 1955
IDBI: 1964
NABARD: 1982
contrast_with: DFIs (long-term finance) vs commercial banks (short-term)

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Candidates may confuse debt trap with conditionality (IMF conditions) or credit denial — the technical definition specifically involves borrowing to pay interest.
elimination_hint: Debt trap = borrowing new loans to pay interest on existing loans, creating a spiral. This is Option B — a precise economic definition.
factual_hooks: Debt trap: when interest payments > revenue growth, requiring new borrowing to service old debt
Sri Lanka 2022 crisis: classic debt trap scenario
contrast_with: Debt trap vs fiscal deficit vs balance of payments crisis
contemporary_relevance: Debt trap diplomacy debate around Chinese BRI loans to developing nations.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Statement 1 (all INSAT satellites launched abroad) is incorrect — some INSAT satellites were launched by ISRO's GSLV. Statement 3 about indigenously built cryogenic engines in GSLV is also wrong — India used Russian cryogenic engines initially.
elimination_hint: Statements 2 (PSLV launched IRS satellites) and 4 (GSAT 2001 with digital broadcast payloads) are correct. Statements 1 and 3 are wrong. Answer is C (1, 2, and 4).
factual_hooks: PSLV: workhorse for IRS satellites
India's indigenous cryogenic engine (CE-7.5) tested much later
GSAT-1 launched 2001 by GSLV-D1
contrast_with: PSLV (polar/sun-synchronous) vs GSLV (geostationary) payloads

subject: Ethics
factual_hooks: Samuel Johnson quote: integrity + knowledge must coexist
Integrity alone is insufficient without competence
contrast_with: Integrity without knowledge (ineffective) vs Knowledge without integrity (dangerous)
mains_intro: Samuel Johnson's maxim highlights the inseparability of integrity and knowledge in creating truly effective and ethical leadership.
mains_body_points: Integrity without knowledge: A well-meaning officer who lacks technical knowledge may make harmful policy decisions despite honest intentions — e.g., poorly designed welfare schemes
Knowledge without integrity: A technically brilliant but corrupt official can cause systemic harm — e.g., a doctor misusing clinical knowledge, or a bureaucrat misusing data
Modern context: AI, biotechnology, nuclear science demand ethical frameworks alongside technical expertise
Civil services application: IAS officers must combine domain knowledge (economics, law) with unwavering ethical commitment
mains_conclusion: Effective governance demands the synthesis of integrity and knowledge — neither alone is sufficient to serve the public good.
contemporary_relevance: Deepfakes, AI misuse, biotech ethics — all illustrate dangers of knowledge without integrity.

subject: History
trap_factor: Candidates may confuse Azad Hind Fauj (Indian National Army, formed 1942 by Mohan Singh) with the Forward Bloc as Bose's creation. Azad Hind Fauj was formed later.
elimination_hint: Subhash Chandra Bose founded the Forward Bloc in 1939 after resigning the Congress presidentship following disagreement with Gandhi over re-election at Tripuri.
factual_hooks: Forward Bloc founded 1939 by Subhash Chandra Bose
Tripuri Congress session (1939): Bose re-elected over Gandhi's candidate Pattabhi Sitaramayya
contrast_with: Forward Bloc (1939, political party) vs INA/Azad Hind Fauj (1942, military force)

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: Statement 1 (UN Charter adopted at Geneva) is wrong — it was adopted at San Francisco. Candidates may accept Geneva due to its association with international organizations.
elimination_hint: Statement 1 is wrong (San Francisco, not Geneva, June 1945). Statements 2 (India in UN 1945) and 3 (Trusteeship Council purpose) are correct. Answer C (2 and 3).
factual_hooks: UN Charter: adopted at San Francisco, June 1945 (not Geneva)
India: founding member of UN 1945
Trusteeship Council: manage former League mandates and colonies
prerequisite_concepts: League of Nations to UN transition
contrast_with: San Francisco (UN founding) vs Geneva (League of Nations HQ, Red Cross)

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Guenter Grass is a Nobel laureate (Literature) not a violinist; Kim Campbell was first female PM of Canada, not associated with literature. Dicky Dolma's Everest record is the distinguishing fact.
elimination_hint: Anchor: Guenter Grass=Nobel Literature (B2), Kim Campbell=First female PM Canada (D1), Dicky Dolma=youngest woman Everest (C4), Tevor Huddleston=anti-apartheid (B3). Answer is B.
factual_hooks: Guenter Grass: German Nobel Prize Literature 1999
Kim Campbell: First female PM Canada
Dicky Dolma: youngest woman to climb Everest

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Ukraine borders Lithuania is a common misconception — they do not share a border; Belarus is between them. Poland, Latvia, Belarus, and Russia border Lithuania.
elimination_hint: Lithuania borders: Poland, Latvia, Belarus, Russia (Kaliningrad). Ukraine does NOT border Lithuania — Belarus and Poland separate them. Answer is B (Ukraine).
factual_hooks: Lithuania's neighbors: Poland, Latvia, Belarus, Russia (Kaliningrad exclave)
Ukraine does not share border with Lithuania
contrast_with: Lithuania borders (Baltic region) vs Ukraine borders (Eastern Europe)
contemporary_relevance: Baltic-Ukraine security corridor gained significance post-2022 Russia-Ukraine conflict.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Candidates may confuse Islamabad's location relative to Peshawar; Peshawar is further north (near Afghanistan border), then Islamabad, then Gujranwala, then Multan going south.
elimination_hint: N to S in Pakistan: Peshawar (NWFP, northernmost) → Islamabad → Gujranwala → Multan (south Punjab). Answer is C.
factual_hooks: Pakistan N→S: Peshawar, Islamabad, Gujranwala, Multan
Peshawar is near Khyber Pass (northernmost major city)
contrast_with: Pakistan N-S sequence vs India N-S city sequences

subject: Internal Security
factual_hooks: Social networking sites: platforms enabling user-generated content and social connections (Facebook, Twitter, LinkedIn)
Key security risks: data privacy, phishing, identity theft, radicalization
contrast_with: Social media security (individual) vs national cyber security (state level)
mains_themes: Social media as security threat vector
mains_intro: Social networking sites have transformed communication but simultaneously created significant security vulnerabilities for individuals, institutions, and nations.
mains_body_points: Definition: platforms enabling user-generated content sharing, networking, and real-time communication (Facebook, Twitter/X, Instagram, LinkedIn)

Security implications: (a) Data privacy breaches — Cambridge Analytica case; (b) Phishing and identity theft; (c) Radicalization and recruitment by terrorist groups; (d) Misinformation/disinformation campaigns affecting elections
National security implications: foreign interference in elections (Russia-US 2016); coordinated inauthentic behavior
India's response: IT Amendment Rules 2021; CERT-In mandatory reporting; intermediary liability framework
mains_conclusion: Balancing free expression and security requires robust regulation, digital literacy, and international cooperation on cyber norms.
contemporary_relevance: Social media manipulation remains a live concern in India's election ecosystem and internal security landscape.

subject: Internal Security
factual_hooks: Cyber warfare: state-sponsored attacks on adversary's digital infrastructure
Stuxnet (2010): first known weaponized cyber attack on Iran's nuclear facility
contrast_with: Cyber warfare (state-sponsored) vs cybercrime (individual/criminal) vs hacktivism
mains_themes: Cyber warfare as modern security threat
mains_intro: Cyber warfare — the use of digital attacks to disrupt, damage, or destroy adversary systems — has emerged as a critical dimension of 21st century conflict.
mains_body_points: Definition: Cyber warfare involves state-sponsored offensive operations targeting adversary's digital infrastructure — power grids, financial systems, defence networks
India's vulnerabilities: critical infrastructure poorly protected; rising incidents (AIIMS Delhi ransomware 2022); border tensions creating cyber espionage risk
Threats: (a) State actors (China, Pakistan); (b) Non-state actors and proxies; (c) Ransomware on critical services

Preparedness: CERT-In (incident response); NCIIPC (critical infrastructure); National Cyber Security Policy 2013; Cyber Surakshit Bharat initiative; but gaps remain in skilled workforce and attribution capability
mains_conclusion: India must urgently strengthen its cyber defence architecture through institutional capacity, international cooperation, and a comprehensive cyber doctrine.
contemporary_relevance: India faces escalating cyber threats from state and non-state actors, underscored by the 2022 AIIMS ransomware attack.

subject: Ethics
factual_hooks: Public servants exercise power over public funds and lives — ethical responsibility is correspondingly high
Second ARC recommended codes of ethics for civil services
contrast_with: Ethical competence vs legal compliance — ethics goes beyond following rules
mains_intro: The elevated ethical responsibility of public servants — arising from their power, public funds, and societal impact — demands continuous personal and institutional investment in ethical development.
mains_body_points: Steps for improving ethical competence: (a) Regular study of ethical frameworks (consequentialism, deontology); (b) Keeping a decision diary to reflect on ethical dilemmas faced
Institutional measures: attending ethics training; mentorship from senior officers with high integrity
Practical actions: following established procedures even under pressure; declaring conflicts of interest; whistle-blowing when necessary
Self-regulation: resisting temptations of power; maintaining transparency in public dealing
mains_conclusion: Ethical competence is not innate but cultivated through deliberate reflection, training, and commitment to public service values.
contemporary_relevance: Ethical lapses by civil servants undermine public trust; PM's emphasis on 'Maximum Governance, Minimum Government' demands high ethical standards.

subject: History
trap_factor: Candidates may doubt whether CST Mumbai is a UNESCO site or confuse Champaner-Pavagadh with non-UNESCO sites; all four listed are actually UNESCO World Heritage Sites.
elimination_hint: All four — Champaner-Pavagadh (2004), CST Mumbai (2004), Mahabalipuram/Mamallapuram (1984), Konark Sun Temple (1984) — are UNESCO World Heritage Sites. Answer is D.
factual_hooks: All four are UNESCO World Heritage Sites of India
CST Mumbai and Champaner-Pavagadh inscribed in 2004
prerequisite_concepts: UNESCO World Heritage Sites in India
contrast_with: UNESCO WHS vs tentative list sites

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Candidates may guess Bangalore (modern) or Hyderabad (Rajiv Gandhi Airport) as the first PPP airport; Cochin is the correct answer as India's first greenfield airport through a public limited company.
elimination_hint: Cochin International Airport Limited (CIAL): India's first airport owned and operated by a public limited company, inaugurated 1999. Answer is B.
factual_hooks: Cochin International Airport: India's first public limited company-owned airport
CIAL formed in 1994; operational 1999
prerequisite_concepts: Airport Authority of India vs private airports
contrast_with: AAI-managed airports vs PPP/private airports

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Candidates may confuse Liberhan Commission with Best Bakery or Tehelka probe given their political salience in the same era.
elimination_hint: Liberhan Commission (1992, reported 2009) probed the demolition of the Babri Masjid at Ayodhya on December 6, 1992. Answer is D.
factual_hooks: Liberhan Commission: established 1992, reported 2009 on Babri Masjid demolition
Took 17 years and held 399 sittings — one of India's longest commissions
contrast_with: Liberhan (Ayodhya) vs Srikrishna Commission (Mumbai riots) vs Nanavati Commission (Gujarat 2002)

subject: History
trap_factor: Kochi's colonial history is complex — Portuguese came first (1503), then Dutch (who defeated Portuguese and built Fort Manuel, not Fort Williams), then British.
elimination_hint: The Dutch defeated the Portuguese and took Kochi in 1663, built Fort Duyts/Cochin Fort (not Fort Williams). Option B correctly identifies Dutch defeating Portuguese in modern Kochi.
factual_hooks: Kochi: Portuguese (1503) → Dutch (1663) → British (1795)
Dutch defeated Portuguese at Kochi — built their own fort
contrast_with: Portuguese vs Dutch vs British colonial presence in Kerala

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Rourkela Steel Plant was set up with West German (not Soviet) collaboration; Soviet collaboration was for Bhilai Steel Plant. This is a classic trap in UPSC.
elimination_hint: Rourkela = West German collaboration; Bhilai = Soviet/Russian collaboration; Durgapur = British collaboration. Statement A is incorrect — answer is A.
factual_hooks: Rourkela Steel Plant: West German collaboration (NOT Soviet)
Bhilai Steel Plant: Soviet collaboration
Durgapur: British collaboration
prerequisite_concepts: First Five Year Plan heavy industry
contrast_with: Rourkela (Germany) vs Bhilai (USSR) vs Durgapur (UK) — Three steel plants, three countries

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Statement 1 says Article 301 pertains to Right to Property — Article 301 is about freedom of trade/commerce, not property (that is Article 300A). Statement 3 says 44th Amendment by Congress — correct party but students may doubt.
elimination_hint: Statement 1 is wrong (Article 301 = freedom of trade, not property). Statement 3 is also questionable — 44th Amendment was by Janata Party government (1978), not Congress. Only Statement 2 (Right to Property = legal right, not fundamental) is correct. Answer is A.
factual_hooks: Right to Property: removed from FR by 44th Amendment (1978, Janata Party), now legal right under Article 300A
Article 301: Freedom of trade and commerce, not property
contrast_with: Article 300A (legal right to property) vs Article 19(1)(f) (former fundamental right)

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Statement 1 says Parliament cannot enlarge SC jurisdiction — Article 138 explicitly allows Parliament to confer additional jurisdiction on SC, making Statement 1 wrong. Statement 2 about administrative expenses is partially correct but has nuances.
elimination_hint: Statement 1 is wrong: Parliament CAN enlarge SC jurisdiction under Article 138. Statement 2 is also not fully accurate — administrative expenses are from Consolidated Fund but appointment nuances differ. Answer is D (neither correct).
factual_hooks: Article 138: Parliament CAN enlarge SC jurisdiction by legislation
SC administrative expenses charged on Consolidated Fund of India (Article 146)
contrast_with: Constitutional jurisdiction vs Parliament-conferred jurisdiction of SC

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Statement 2 says Kyoto Protocol deals primarily with ozone layer depletion — this is wrong (that is Montreal Protocol); Kyoto deals with greenhouse gases/climate change.
elimination_hint: Statement 1 (Kyoto in force 2005) and Statement 3 (methane more harmful than CO2 per molecule) are both correct. Statement 2 is wrong — ozone is Montreal Protocol's domain. Answer is B.
factual_hooks: Kyoto Protocol: entered into force February 2005, deals with GHGs
Methane: GWP ~25x higher than CO2 over 100 years
Montreal Protocol: deals with ozone-depleting substances
contrast_with: Kyoto (climate/GHG) vs Montreal (ozone) protocols

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Students may place North America above Africa in land area, forgetting that Africa (30.3 million sq km) is the world's second largest continent after Asia.
elimination_hint: By area: Asia > Africa > North America > South America > Antarctica > Europe > Australia. Among options: Africa > North America > South America > Europe. Answer is B.
factual_hooks: Continent area ranking: Asia, Africa, N.America, S.America, Antarctica, Europe, Australia
Africa: ~30.3 million sq km; N. America: ~24.7 million sq km
contrast_with: Area ranking vs population ranking of continents

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Bondle Wildlife Sanctuary is in Goa (not Assam); Orang Sanctuary is in Assam; Kangerghat is in Chhattisgarh; Ushakothi is in Odisha. These state associations are frequently mixed.
elimination_hint: Anchor on Orang Sanctuary = Assam (well-known rhino sanctuary). Kangerghat = Chhattisgarh (also known). Bondle = Goa; Ushakothi = Odisha. Answer is B (A-4, B-3, C-2, D-1).
factual_hooks: Orang National Park: Assam (one-horned rhino habitat)
Kangerghat NP: Chhattisgarh
Bondle WS: Goa
Ushakothi WS: Odisha
contrast_with: Orang (Assam) vs Kaziranga (Assam) — both rhino habitats

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Candidates assume Lok Adalats handle only motor accident and consumer disputes; mobile cellular disputes and pension cases are also part of their jurisdiction.
elimination_hint: Lok Adalats can handle all three categories: mobile cellular disputes (under TRAI/consumer), motor accidents, and pension cases. Answer is D (1, 2, and 3).
factual_hooks: Lok Adalats: established under Legal Services Authorities Act 1987
Jurisdiction: motor accidents, matrimonial disputes, labour, pension, compoundable offences, telecom disputes
contrast_with: Lok Adalats (consensual, no appeal) vs civil courts (adversarial)

subject: History
trap_factor: Annie Besant was a Fabian socialist before becoming active in Indian nationalist politics; candidates may associate her only with INC or Theosophy and miss her Fabian roots.
elimination_hint: Annie Besant was a prominent Fabian Society member in Britain before her Indian phase. R. Palme Dutt was a Communist, not Fabian. Select A.
factual_hooks: Annie Besant: Fabian Socialist in UK before moving to India
Fabian Society: British socialist organization advocating gradual reform (not revolution)
contrast_with: Fabianism (gradual reform) vs Marxism (revolutionary change)

subject: History
trap_factor: Candidates may associate Ghadar with 'Central America' due to its global reach, but the movement was headquartered in San Francisco, USA — North America.
elimination_hint: Ghadar Party (1913) was founded in San Francisco by Lala Har Dayal; active among Indian diaspora in North America (USA and Canada). Answer is B.
factual_hooks: Ghadar Party: founded 1913, San Francisco, North America
Led by Lala Har Dayal; targeted Indian soldiers in WWI to revolt
contrast_with: Ghadar (overseas revolution) vs INC (constitutional agitation) vs revolutionary groups in India

subject: History
trap_factor: Candidates may remember Congress ministries formed in 7 provinces (1937) but forget that Punjab was not among them due to the Unionist Party's dominance there.
elimination_hint: Congress formed ministries in 7 provinces (1937) under GoI Act 1935: Madras, Bombay, Bengal (briefly), UP, Bihar, CP, Orissa. Punjab had Muslim League-Unionist coalition. Answer is D.
factual_hooks: 1937 elections: Congress formed governments in 7 provinces; NOT in Punjab, Sind, Bengal, Assam initially
Punjab: Unionist Party dominant — Congress did not form ministry
contrast_with: Provinces where Congress formed govt vs where it did not (Punjab, Sind)

subject: History
trap_factor: Candidates may confuse Lahore session with Bombay session (1934, when Civil Disobedience was formally suspended) or Lucknow (1936, Nehru's presidentship).
elimination_hint: Lahore Congress Session (December 1929) under Nehru authorized Civil Disobedience and passed Poorna Swaraj resolution. The actual launch of Civil Disobedience (Dandi March) followed in March 1930.
factual_hooks: Lahore Congress 1929: Poorna Swaraj resolution + authorization of Civil Disobedience
Dandi March (March 1930): actual launch of Civil Disobedience Movement
contrast_with: Lahore (1929, authorization) vs actual CDM launch (1930, Dandi March)

subject: History
trap_factor: Jawaharlal Nehru was NOT among the founders of UP Kisan Sabha (Feb 1918) — this is a trap since Nehru was prominent in peasant politics in UP later (Pratapgarh/Rae Bareli 1920).
elimination_hint: UP Kisan Sabha (1918) was founded by Indra Narain Dwivedi, Gauri Shankar Misra, and Madan Mohan Malviya. Nehru's peasant involvement came later (1920 Awadh movement). Answer is C.
factual_hooks: UP Kisan Sabha 1918: founded by Indra Narain Dwivedi, Gauri Shankar Misra, Madan Mohan Malviya
Nehru's kisan engagement: Awadh peasant movement 1920, not 1918
contrast_with: UP Kisan Sabha (1918) vs Awadh Kisan Sabha (1920) — different organizations

subject: History
trap_factor: Candidates may confuse Kunwar Singh with UP leaders since the 1857 revolt is often associated with UP centers like Lucknow and Kanpur.
elimination_hint: Kunwar Singh of Jagdishpur, Bihar — an 80-year-old zamindar who led the revolt in Bihar — is a well-known figure. Answer is A (Bihar).
factual_hooks: Kunwar Singh: leader from Jagdishpur, Arrah district, Bihar in 1857 revolt
Led revolt at 80 years of age — known for military strategy against British
contrast_with: Kunwar Singh (Bihar) vs Tantia Tope (Central India) vs Nana Sahib (UP)

subject: History
trap_factor: Candidates may include Chittor (Chittorgarh) in 1857 revolt as it was a historical Rajput stronghold, but Rajputana generally stayed loyal to the British in 1857.
elimination_hint: Chittor/Chittorgarh (Rajasthan) was not affected by the 1857 revolt — Rajputana rulers largely supported the British. Jhansi, Jagdishpur (Bihar), and Lucknow were all major centres. Answer is B.
factual_hooks: 1857 revolt major centres: Delhi, Meerut, Kanpur, Lucknow, Jhansi, Arrah/Jagdishpur
Rajputana: largely remained loyal to British in 1857
contrast_with: Revolt-affected areas vs loyal princely states in 1857

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Statement 2 says Article 243Q envisages only Municipal Council and Municipal Corporation — it actually envisages three types (Nagar Panchayat, Municipal Council, Municipal Corporation), making it incorrect.
elimination_hint: Statement 1 is correct (Part IX, 73rd Amendment). Statement 2 is wrong — Article 243Q provides for three types of municipalities, not two. Answer is A (1 only).
factual_hooks: 73rd Amendment (1992): inserted Part IX on Panchayats
74th Amendment: Part IX-A on municipalities; Article 243Q: three types — Nagar Panchayat, Municipal Council, Municipal Corporation
prerequisite_concepts: Panchayati Raj and urban local bodies
contrast_with: 73rd Amendment (rural Panchayats) vs 74th Amendment (urban municipalities)

subject: Ethics
factual_hooks: Crisis of conscience: moral conflict between duty, personal values, and social norms
Common in civil servants: balancing orders vs ethics
contrast_with: Crisis of conscience (internal moral conflict) vs ethical dilemma (competing external obligations)
mains_intro: A 'crisis of conscience' arises when one's inner moral compass conflicts with external pressures — be they orders, social expectations, or personal gain.
mains_body_points: Definition: Crisis of conscience occurs when a person is pulled between their deeply held values and external demands — career pressure, peer influence, or authority
Example: A civil servant directed by superiors to approve a substandard project that benefits a politically connected firm — following orders conflicts with duty to public welfare
Resolution approach: (a) Consult legal/ethical framework; (b) Seek guidance from mentor; (c) Document dissent formally; (d) If necessary, invoke whistle-blower protection
Inner peace as a measure: resolution is achieved when the decision can withstand public scrutiny and personal reflection
mains_conclusion: Resolving a crisis of conscience demands moral courage — the willingness to act on one's values even at personal cost.
contemporary_relevance: Civil servants increasingly face crises of conscience in implementing politically driven decisions that may conflict with public interest.

subject: History
trap_factor: Kamakhya Temple (Assam) and Sri Mariamman Temple (Singapore/Tamil Nadu) might be confused with Suryavarman II's grand temple; Angkor Vat is the definitive answer.
elimination_hint: Angkor Vat in Cambodia was commissioned by Khmer King Suryavarman II in the 12th century CE — one of the world's largest religious monuments. Answer is B.
factual_hooks: Angkor Vat: built by Suryavarman II (1113–1150 CE), Cambodia
Originally a Hindu temple to Vishnu, later became Buddhist
prerequisite_concepts: Hindu temple architecture in Southeast Asia
related_concepts: Indian cultural influence in Southeast Asia
contrast_with: Angkor Vat (Khmer/Suryavarman II) vs Borobudur (Sailendra dynasty/Buddhist)

subject: Ethics
factual_hooks: Gandhi's trusteeship concept: wealthy should act as trustees of wealth for society
Sustainable development: use resources for need, not limitless consumption
contrast_with: Gandhi's trusteeship (ethical restraint) vs market capitalism (profit maximization)
mains_intro: Gandhi's timeless insight that the earth provides enough for every person's need but not their greed encapsulates the ethics of sustainable, equitable resource use.
mains_body_points: The quote distinguishes between 'need' (essential, finite) and 'greed' (unlimited, destructive) — a distinction central to environmental and economic ethics
Present context: overconsumption and climate change represent greed overwhelming the planet's capacity; global inequity shows needs of billions unmet while few over-consume
Trusteeship theory: Gandhi argued the wealthy are custodians, not owners, of their wealth — applicable to corporate social responsibility today
SDGs and circular economy: modern frameworks echo Gandhi's principle — 'enough for all' through sustainable production and distribution
mains_conclusion: Gandhi's quote remains a moral compass for sustainable development — reminding humanity that ecological integrity and social equity are inseparable.
contemporary_relevance: Climate crisis, inequality, and over-exploitation of natural resources make this quote highly relevant to contemporary governance.

subject: Economy
factual_hooks: India's defence FDI cap raised to 74% automatic route, 100% through government approval
Defence manufacturing is a key component of 'Make in India'
contrast_with: FDI-led defence vs indigenous R&D driven defence (DRDO)
mains_themes: Short vs long-term economic and security implications
mains_intro: Liberalization of FDI in India's defence sector holds transformative potential for both national security capabilities and economic growth.
mains_body_points: Short-run benefits: Technology inflows, employment generation, reduced import bill, industrial base development
Short-run concerns: Risk of foreign control over sensitive technologies; potential crowding out of domestic MSMEs
Long-run benefits: Development of indigenous defence industrial ecosystem; defence exports (India targets $5 billion by 2025); spin-off technologies for civilian use
Long-run concerns: Strategic dependence if technology transfer is partial; need for strong IP protections and offset policy
Economic impact: Multiplier effect on allied industries (electronics, metals, aerospace)
mains_conclusion: Calibrated FDI liberalization in defence, with robust regulatory safeguards and technology transfer mandates, can transform India into a net defence exporter while ensuring self-reliance.
contemporary_relevance: India's defence export ambitions and Atmanirbhar Bharat make this question highly current.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Candidates may guess Germany or USA due to their large nuclear plants; Japan's Kashiwazaki-Kariwa was the world's largest nuclear plant, affected by 2007 earthquake.
elimination_hint: The 2007 earthquake that caused radioactive water leakage at the Kashiwazaki-Kariwa Nuclear Power Plant (world's largest) occurred in Japan. Answer is C.
factual_hooks: Kashiwazaki-Kariwa: Japan, world's largest nuclear power plant by capacity
2007 Chuetsu offshore earthquake caused radioactive water leakage
contrast_with: Kashiwazaki-Kariwa (2007) vs Fukushima (2011) — two separate Japan nuclear incidents

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Candidates may classify ticks and mites as insects due to their similar ecological role; they are arachnids (8 legs) not insects (6 legs).
elimination_hint: Ticks and mites belong to class Arachnida (8 legs, 2 body segments). Insects have 6 legs. Crustaceans include crabs/shrimp. Myriapods are centipedes/millipedes.
factual_hooks: Ticks and mites: Class Arachnida, Order Acari
Arachnids: 8 legs, 2 body parts — unlike insects (6 legs, 3 parts)
contrast_with: Arachnids (ticks, spiders) vs Insects (mosquitoes, flies) vs Crustaceans (crabs)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Statement B says addition of solutes increases water potential — the opposite is true; solutes lower water potential, enabling osmosis from high to low water potential regions.
elimination_hint: Option B is incorrect: adding solutes DECREASES water potential (by diluting the chemical potential of water). The other statements about boiling point elevation, vapor pressure depression, and osmotic pressure are all correct.
factual_hooks: Water potential: decreases with addition of solutes (solute potential becomes more negative)
Boiling point elevation and vapor pressure depression are colligative properties
contrast_with: Water potential vs solute potential vs pressure potential

subject: History
trap_factor: Candidates may confuse Friedrich Engels with Emile Durkheim (sociologist) or Max Weber (sociologist) — all were European social theorists but only Engels co-authored the Communist Manifesto.
elimination_hint: The Communist Manifesto (1848) was co-authored by Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels. Durkheim and Weber were sociologists, not co-authors of Marxist texts.
factual_hooks: Communist Manifesto (1848): co-authored by Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels
Engels also co-wrote 'Das Kapital' volume 2 and 3 after Marx's death
contrast_with: Marx and Engels (Communist Manifesto) vs Durkheim (social solidarity) vs Weber (bureaucracy)
